title: Sampling Methods
examTip: Examiners often ask you to suggest an appropriate sampling method for a specific study and justify it — know when each method is best used, not just the definitions.
introTips: Population = everyone the researcher is interested in (not just 'people').
Representative sample = reflects the characteristics of the population.
Larger samples are more likely to be representative — but cost more.
intro: If you want to understand the views of all secondary school pupils in England, you can't ask every single one of them — there are over three million. So sociologists choose a sample: a smaller group they hope reflects the wider population. The method used to select that sample shapes everything — a badly chosen sample can make even the most sophisticated analysis completely meaningless.

keyPoints.heading: Population, Sample, and Sampling Frame
keyPoints.content: In sociological research, the population is the entire group the researcher is interested in — not necessarily 'people' in the general sense. It could be all secondary school pupils in England, all working-class women in Birmingham, all convicted drug offenders in the UK, or all GP surgeries in London. Populations are almost always too large to study in their entirety, so a sample is selected. The goal is a representative sample: one that accurately reflects the key characteristics of the population (age, gender, class, ethnicity, etc.) so that findings can be generalised — applied back to the wider population. The sampling frame is the list from which the sample is drawn — for example: a school register (if studying pupils at one school), the electoral roll (if studying voters), or a phone directory. The sampling frame must be up to date and as complete as possible — gaps in the frame (people not on the list) introduce bias before the study has even begun.
writeBullets: Population — the entire group the researcher wants to study (not just 'people')
Sample — a smaller group selected to represent the population
Representative sample — accurately reflects the population's key characteristics
Generalisation — applying findings from the sample to the wider population
Sampling frame — the list from which the sample is drawn (must be complete and current)
Gaps in the sampling frame introduce bias before research begins
keyPoints.memTip: POP-FRAME-SAMPLE: define your population → find a frame → select your sample.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say 'population means all people' — correct this early. It means the group the researcher defines as relevant.

keyPoints.heading: Random and Systematic Sampling
keyPoints.content: Random sampling: every member of the sampling frame has an equal chance of being selected. In practice: names drawn from a hat, or a random number generator assigns numbers to names and selects them. True random sampling is unbiased — no individual's characteristics influence their chance of selection. However, by chance a random sample may still be unrepresentative (e.g., an unlucky draw might produce a sample that is 80% male). Systematic random sampling: select every nth name from the sampling frame. For example, to select 100 from a list of 1,000: pick a random starting point, then select every 10th name. More practical than true random sampling when the frame is large. Advantages of both: objective and unbiased (personal judgement doesn't influence selection), replicable, and easy to explain. Disadvantages: both require a complete and accurate sampling frame (which may not exist); they may not guarantee representation of specific minority subgroups (a random sample of 100 from a school might include no students with disabilities, even if they make up 5% of the school).
writeBullets: Random sampling — every member of the sampling frame has an equal chance of selection
Can still be unrepresentative by chance despite being unbiased
Systematic random sampling — select every nth name from the sampling frame
Both are objective, unbiased, and replicable
Both require a complete and accurate sampling frame
May not guarantee representation of small minority subgroups
keyPoints.memTip: Random = equal chance for everyone. Systematic = count along the list at regular intervals.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Random ≠ haphazard. True random sampling is methodically unbiased — contrast with opportunity/volunteer sampling.

keyPoints.heading: Stratified, Snowball, and Other Sampling Methods
keyPoints.content: Stratified sampling: divide the population into relevant subgroups (strata) — for example, by gender, social class, ethnicity, or age — then randomly sample proportionally within each stratum. If a school is 60% female and 40% male, the sample should match these proportions. More representative than simple random sampling for the key variables chosen. Purposive (deliberate) sampling: the researcher deliberately selects cases that fit specific research criteria — e.g., selecting only people who have experienced homelessness. Useful for qualitative research where you want particular types of participant. Snowball sampling: begin with one or a small number of participants who then recommend others from the same group. Particularly useful for hard-to-reach groups such as undocumented migrants, drug users, sex workers, or gang members — people who would never appear on a conventional sampling frame. Major disadvantage: produces a non-representative, biased sample (each recommendation is based on personal connections). Volunteer (opportunity) sampling: use whoever is available or volunteers to participate — cheap and easy but almost certainly unrepresentative (volunteers differ from non-volunteers in systematic ways).
writeBullets: Stratified sampling — divide into subgroups (strata), sample proportionally within each
More representative than simple random for key variables like gender and class
Purposive sampling — deliberately select cases fitting research criteria
Snowball sampling — participants recommend others; useful for hard-to-reach groups
Snowball disadvantage — non-representative; based on personal connections
Volunteer/opportunity sampling — uses whoever is available; easy but rarely representative
keyPoints.memTip: SRSP — Stratified (best for representation), Random (equal chance), Snowball (hard-to-reach), Purposive (specific criteria).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Snowball is frequently tested because it applies to sensitive/hidden populations — link it to ethical research contexts.

keyPoints.heading: Pilot Studies and the Problem of Representativeness
keyPoints.content: A pilot study is a small-scale rehearsal of the full research before the main study is conducted. It allows the researcher to identify problems with: question wording (are questions understood as intended?), interview technique (does the interviewer inadvertently lead?), observation access (will the gatekeeper cooperate?), timing (how long does the study actually take?), and feasibility (is the chosen method workable in practice?). The pilot study allows refinement of methods before expensive resources are committed to the full study. Representativeness: even with the best sampling method, a sample may not be representative. Key threats: the sampling method itself (non-random methods introduce bias), response rate (low return rates mean non-responders — who may differ systematically from responders — are excluded), and self-selection bias (people who choose to participate differ from those who don't — volunteers tend to be more engaged, more educated, more interested in the topic). Generalisation: quantitative research typically aims to generalise findings to the wider population; interpretive/qualitative research may not claim generalisability but instead offers depth, detail, and insight into particular social situations.
writeBullets: Pilot study — small-scale test of methods before the full study to identify problems
Allows refinement of questions, technique, access, and timing before full commitment
Representativeness — whether the sample accurately reflects the population
Response rate — who doesn't respond may differ from who does (biased sample)
Self-selection bias — volunteers differ from non-volunteers
Qualitative research may not claim generalisability but offers depth and insight instead
keyPoints.memTip: Pilot = test flight before the real thing. Always do one — it saves disasters later.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Pilot studies are often overlooked in answers — reward students who include them as good research practice.

tables.title: Sampling Methods: Comparison
rows: Random
Every member of sampling frame has equal chance (lottery/random number)
Unbiased; objective; replicable
May miss minority groups by chance; needs complete sampling frame
Population is relatively homogeneous and a complete frame exists
Systematic random
Select every nth name from the frame
Practical for large frames; unbiased; easy to apply
Needs complete frame; minority groups may be missed
Large sampling frame exists; quick and objective selection needed
Stratified
Divide into subgroups (strata) then randomly sample proportionally within each
More representative for key variables; ensures minority groups included
Requires knowledge of population characteristics; more complex to organise
Researcher wants to ensure representation of specific subgroups
Snowball
Start with one participant who recommends others from their network
Accesses hard-to-reach, hidden, or stigmatised groups
Non-representative; biased by personal networks; hard to generalise
Studying hidden groups (drug users, undocumented migrants, gang members)
Purposive
Researcher deliberately selects cases fitting specific criteria
Gets exactly who the researcher needs for their question
Not representative; researcher's judgement shapes who is included
Qualitative research requiring specific types of participant
Volunteer/Opportunity
Use whoever is available or willing to take part
Cheap and quick; no sampling frame needed
Very likely to be unrepresentative; volunteers differ from non-volunteers
Preliminary studies; when access is severely limited (not ideal)

1.question: A sociologist wants to study the experiences of undocumented migrants living in London. Which sampling method is most appropriate and why?
options: Snowball sampling — undocumented migrants won't appear on any sampling frame and can only be reached through personal recommendations
Random sampling — gives everyone an equal chance of being selected
Stratified sampling — ensures the sample reflects the proportions in the population
Systematic sampling — selecting every nth name from the electoral roll
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Undocumented migrants are a hidden, hard-to-reach group — they won't be on any official list. Snowball sampling (starting with one contact who recommends others) is the only realistic approach, even though it produces a non-representative sample.
1.wrongFeedback: Think about why undocumented migrants can't be reached by conventional methods. They won't appear on any electoral roll, register, or directory. Which method is designed specifically for groups like this?

3.question: What is the main purpose of conducting a pilot study before the full research?
options: To identify and fix problems with questions, technique, and access before committing full resources
To collect all the data needed for the final research report
To select the most representative sample from the population
To ensure the research will produce quantitative rather than qualitative data
3.correctFeedback: Correct. A pilot study is a test run — it reveals problems with wording, timing, access, and technique before the full study begins, allowing improvements that save time and money.
3.wrongFeedback: A pilot study is not the main study — it's a small-scale rehearsal. What would be the point of doing a small version before the big version?

commonMistakes: Saying 'population means all the people' — in sociology 'population' means the specific group the researcher defines (could be all left-handed GCSE students, all care home workers, etc.).
Thinking random sampling guarantees representativeness — it removes bias in the selection process, but a random sample can still be unrepresentative by chance.
Confusing snowball with opportunity sampling — snowball uses recommendations from within the group; opportunity just uses whoever happens to be available.
Forgetting that stratified sampling requires knowledge of the population's characteristics before you can stratify — if you don't know how many of each group exist, you can't sample proportionally.
Not mentioning the pilot study when asked about research design — it's a mark that students regularly leave on the table.
examTips: When asked to choose a sampling method, always justify your choice by linking it to the specific population and research question — don't just name the method.
For hard-to-reach populations (gang members, drug users, undocumented migrants), always suggest snowball sampling and explain why conventional methods won't work.
Mention the pilot study when describing how a study should be carried out — it demonstrates good methodological awareness.
revisionCardBullets: Population — the entire group the researcher is interested in (defined specifically, not 'all people')
Sample — a smaller, selected group intended to represent the population
Sampling frame — the list from which the sample is drawn (must be complete and up to date)
Representative sample — one that accurately reflects the population's key characteristics
Random sampling — equal chance of selection; unbiased but needs complete frame
Systematic random — select every nth name; practical for large frames
Stratified sampling — sample proportionally within subgroups (strata); most representative
Snowball sampling — participants recommend others; best for hard-to-reach hidden groups
Purposive sampling — deliberate selection of cases fitting specific criteria
Volunteer/opportunity sampling — uses whoever is available; rarely representative
Pilot study — small-scale test to identify and fix problems before the full study
Self-selection bias — volunteers differ from non-volunteers; threatens representativeness
